name: Maero
description: The Maero are wild, man-like creatures from Māori mythology, often described as fierce and dangerous beings. They are said to be covered in coarse hair, with long, bony fingers tipped with sharp claws or nails that they use to kill and dismember their victims. Maero are associated with the deep forest and remote mountain regions, living in caves or rough shelters. They are believed to be cannibals and possess immense strength, capable of uprooting trees and moving large boulders. Some accounts suggest they are skilled craftspeople, able to create tools and weapons from stone, though these are typically used for hunting and warfare. They are often depicted as being hostile towards humans and are a constant threat in traditional Māori narratives, embodying the untamed and dangerous aspects of the natural world. Their existence serves as a cautionary tale, emphasizing the importance of respecting the boundaries between civilization and the wilderness. They are considered to be ancient inhabitants of Aotearoa (New Zealand), predating the arrival of the Māori people.
subtitle: Wild, hairy, man-eating forest dwellers
appearance: Maero are described as being large and muscular, covered in thick, coarse hair ranging in color from brown to black. They possess long, bony fingers tipped with sharp claws or abnormally long fingernails used for tearing flesh. Their faces are often depicted as grotesque, with prominent teeth and wild, staring eyes. Some accounts suggest they have abnormally long arms and legs, adapted for navigating the dense forests they inhabit. They are generally naked or wear minimal coverings made from animal hides or woven plant fibers.
habitat: Maero inhabit the deep forests, remote mountain regions, and caves of Aotearoa (New Zealand). They prefer areas that are far from human settlements and offer ample cover and resources for hunting. Their lairs are often located in difficult-to-reach places, reflecting their preference for isolation and their ability to navigate treacherous terrain.
behavior: Maero are fiercely territorial and aggressive, often attacking humans and other creatures that venture into their domain. They are primarily hunters and are known for their cannibalistic tendencies. They are nocturnal creatures, preferring to hunt under the cover of darkness. While generally solitary, they may occasionally form small family groups or bands. They are considered cunning and resourceful, able to track prey and set traps. They communicate through guttural roars and howls.

keyMyths.title: The Maero and the Traveler
keyMyths.description: A lone traveler encounters a Maero in the forest and must use their wit and courage to escape the creature's clutches. The tale highlights the Maero's ferocity and the dangers of the wilderness.

sources.author: Sir George Grey
sources.description: A compilation of Māori myths and legends collected by Grey during his time as Governor of New Zealand.
sources.text: A compilation of Māori myths and legends collected by Grey during his time as Governor of New Zealand.

sources.author: Margaret Orbell
sources.work: The Illustrated Encyclopedia of Māori Myth and Legend
sources.description: A comprehensive overview of Māori mythology, including detailed descriptions of various creatures and deities.
